                temp_d: float = 1.0, hard_kappa: Optional[float] = None) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, Dict[str, torch.Tensor]]:
        H_list = precompute_multi_hop(P, X, self.L)
        S = self.group_head(X)
        C = self.compat()
        M_list = diffuse_groups(P, S, self.L)
        if self.risk_mode == 'js':
            R = compute_js_risk(S, C, M_list)
        else:
            A = compute_base_alignment(S, C, M_list)
            R = compute_risk_from_alignment(A)
        D = compute_denoising_proxy(H_list, temperature=temp_d)
        hard_gate = (R > hard_kappa) if hard_kappa is not None else None
        Z, w, lam = self.hop_attn(H_list, R, D, rho, hard_gate)
        logits = self.pred(Z + X)  # residual from X
        aux = {'S': S, 'C': C, 'R': R, 'D': D, 'w': w, 'lambda': lam, 'H_list': H_list}
        return logits, aux

# ---------------------------------------------
# Budgets ρ_i
# ---------------------------------------------

@torch.no_grad()
def build_budget(P: torch.sparse.FloatTensor, S: torch.Tensor, rho0: float, rho_min: float, rho_max: float, alpha: float = 0.7) -> torch.Tensor:
    maxS = S.max(dim=1).values
    b1 = 1.0 - maxS
    S1 = torch.sparse.mm(P, S)
    b2 = 1.0 - (S * S1).sum(dim=1)
    b = alpha * b1 + (1 - alpha) * b2
    b = (b - b.min()) / (b.max() - b.min() + 1e-6)
    rho = (rho0 * b).clamp(rho_min, rho_max)
    return rho

# ---------------------------------------------
# Baselines: SIGN uniform and SIGN attention (risk-agnostic)
# ---------------------------------------------

class SIGNUniform(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim: int, hidden: int, out_dim: int, L: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.L = L
        self.f = nn.Linear(in_dim, hidden)
        self.pred = nn.Linear(hidden, out_dim)

    def forward(self, H_list: List[torch.Tensor]):
        Fk = [self.f(Hk) for Hk in H_list]
        Z = torch.stack(Fk, dim=1).mean(dim=1)
        logits = self.pred(Z)
        return logits

class SIGNAttnSimple(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim: int, hidden: int, out_dim: int, L: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.L = L
        self.f = nn.Linear(in_dim, hidden)
        self.theta = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(L + 1))
        self.pred = nn.Linear(hidden, out_dim)

    def forward(self, H_list: List[torch.Tensor]):
        Fk = [self.f(Hk) for Hk in H_list]
        s = self.theta.view(1, -1)
        w = torch.softmax(s, dim=1)  # global hop weights, risk-agnostic
        Z = (w.unsqueeze(-1) * torch.stack(Fk, dim=1)).sum(dim=1)
        logits = self.pred(Z)
        return logits, w.squeeze(0)

# ---------------------------------------------
# Training helpers
# ---------------------------------------------

def accuracy_from_logits(logits: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, idx: torch.Tensor) -> float:
    pred = logits.argmax(dim=1)
    return (pred[idx] == y[idx]).float().mean().item()

def train_migad(P, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                L=8, G=16, hidden=64, top_rho=4, rank=8, risk_mode='align',
                epochs=300, warmup_epochs=80, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4,
                rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.5, lambda_mix=1.0, device='cpu',
                eval_every=10, hard_kappa_warm=None, hard_kappa_cool=0.9,
                verbose=True):
    N, d_in = X.size()
    num_classes = int(y.max().item()) + 1
    model = MiGAD(d_in, hidden, num_classes, G, L, top_rho=top_rho, rank=rank, risk_mode=risk_mode).to(device)
    model.group_head.init_prototypes(X)
    X = X.to(device); y = y.to(device)
    P = P.coalesce().to(device)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr, weight_decay=wd)

    best_val = -1e9
    best = {}
    losses = []

    for epoch in range(1, epochs + 1):
        model.train(); opt.zero_grad()
        with torch.no_grad():
            S_now = model.group_head(X)
            rho = build_budget(P, S_now, rho0=rho0, rho_min=rho_min, rho_max=rho_max)
        temp_d = 1.0 if epoch < warmup_epochs else 0.6
        hard_kappa = hard_kappa_warm if epoch < warmup_epochs else hard_kappa_cool
        logits, aux = model(P, X, rho, temp_d=temp_d, hard_kappa=hard_kappa)
        loss_task = F.cross_entropy(logits[idx_train], y[idx_train])
        cons = (aux['w'] * aux['R']).sum(dim=1)
        viol = torch.clamp(cons - rho, min=0.0)
        mix_pen = viol.pow(2).mean()
        S = aux['S']; C = aux['C']
        ent_S = -(S.clamp_min(1e-8) * S.clamp_min(1e-8).log()).sum(dim=1).mean()
        kl_C_uniform = (C * (C.clamp_min(1e-8) * C.size(1)).log()).sum(dim=1).mean()
        coef_mix = (0.1 if epoch < warmup_epochs else lambda_mix)
        loss = loss_task + coef_mix * mix_pen + 1e-3 * ent_S + 1e-4 * kl_C_uniform
        loss.backward()
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 5.0)
        opt.step()

        losses.append(loss.item())

        if epoch % eval_every == 0 or epoch == 1:
            model.eval()
            with torch.no_grad():
                logits_eval, aux_eval = model(P, X, rho)
            acc_tr = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y, idx_train)
            acc_v = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y, idx_val)
            acc_te = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y, idx_test)
            if verbose:
                print(f"[MiGAD][Epoch {epoch:03d}] loss={loss.item():.4f} task={loss_task.item():.4f} mix_pen={mix_pen.item():.4f} | acc (tr/val/te) = {acc_tr:.3f}/{acc_v:.3f}/{acc_te:.3f}")
            if acc_v > best_val:
                best_val = acc_v
                best = {
                    'state': {k: v.detach().cpu() for k, v in model.state_dict().items()},
                    'val': acc_v, 'test': acc_te, 'rho': rho.detach().cpu(), 'aux': {k: v.detach().cpu() for k, v in aux_eval.items()},
                    'losses': losses
                }
    print(f"[MiGAD] Best val={best.get('val', 0):.3f}, test={best.get('test', 0):.3f}")
    return best


def train_sign_uniform(P, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                       L=8, hidden=64, epochs=300, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4, device='cpu', eval_every=10, verbose=True):
    N, d_in = X.size()
    num_classes = int(y.max().item()) + 1
    X = X.to(device); y = y.to(device)
    P = P.coalesce().to(device)
    H_list = precompute_multi_hop(P, X, L)

    model = SIGNUniform(d_in, hidden, num_classes, L).to(device)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr, weight_decay=wd)
    best_val = -1e9
    best = {}
    losses = []
    for epoch in range(1, epochs + 1):
        model.train(); opt.zero_grad()
        logits = model(H_list)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits[idx_train], y[idx_train])
        loss.backward(); opt.step()
        losses.append(loss.item())
        if epoch % eval_every == 0 or epoch == 1:
            model.eval(); logits_eval = model(H_list)
            acc_tr = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y, idx_train)
            acc_v = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y, idx_val)
            acc_te = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y, idx_test)
            if verbose:
                print(f"[SIGN-Uniform][Epoch {epoch:03d}] loss={loss.item():.4f} | acc (tr/val/te) = {acc_tr:.3f}/{acc_v:.3f}/{acc_te:.3f}")
            if acc_v > best_val:
                best_val = acc_v
                best = {'state': {k: v.detach().cpu() for k, v in model.state_dict().items()}, 'val': acc_v, 'test': acc_te, 'losses': losses}
    print(f"[SIGN-Uniform] Best val={best.get('val', 0):.3f}, test={best.get('test', 0):.3f}")
    return best


def train_sign_attn(P, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                    L=8, hidden=64, epochs=300, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4, device='cpu', eval_every=10, verbose=True):
    N, d_in = X.size()
    num_classes = int(y.max().item()) + 1
    X = X.to(device); y = y.to(device)
    P = P.coalesce().to(device)
    H_list = precompute_multi_hop(P, X, L)

    model = SIGNAttnSimple(d_in, hidden, num_classes, L).to(device)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr, weight_decay=wd)
    best_val = -1e9
    best = {}
    losses = []
    for epoch in range(1, epochs + 1):
        model.train(); opt.zero_grad()
        logits, w = model(H_list)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(logits[idx_train], y[idx_train])
        loss.backward(); opt.step()
        losses.append(loss.item())
        if epoch % eval_every == 0 or epoch == 1:
            model.eval(); logits_eval, w_eval = model(H_list)
            acc_tr = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y, idx_train)
            acc_v = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y, idx_val)
            acc_te = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y, idx_test)
            if verbose:
                print(f"[SIGN-Attn][Epoch {epoch:03d}] loss={loss.item():.4f} | acc (tr/val/te) = {acc_tr:.3f}/{acc_v:.3f}/{acc_te:.3f}")
            if acc_v > best_val:
                best_val = acc_v
                best = {'state': {k: v.detach().cpu() for k, v in model.state_dict().items()}, 'val': acc_v, 'test': acc_te, 'w': w_eval.detach().cpu(), 'losses': losses}
    print(f"[SIGN-Attn] Best val={best.get('val', 0):.3f}, test={best.get('test', 0):.3f}")
    return best

# ---------------------------------------------
# Data generation: SBM + utilities
# ---------------------------------------------

def generate_sbm(N: int = 1000, d: int = 64, K: int = 5, p_in: float = 0.08, p_out: float = 0.01, feat_snr: float = 2.0, seed: int = 1):
    """
    Create an undirected SBM graph with Gaussian features.
    Returns: X [N,d], y [N], edge_index [2,E] (both directions for undirected)
    """
    set_seed(seed)
    # Class sizes (balanced)
    n_per = [N // K] * K
    for i in range(N % K):
        n_per[i] += 1
    y = torch.empty(N, dtype=torch.long)
    start = 0
    for c, n in enumerate(n_per):
        y[start:start+n] = c
        start += n
    perm = torch.randperm(N)
    y = y[perm]
    # Block edges
    idx_by_class = [ (y == c).nonzero(as_tuple=False).view(-1) for c in range(K) ]
    edges = []
    for a in range(K):
        Ia = idx_by_class[a]
        for b in range(a, K):
            Ib = idx_by_class[b]
            pa = p_in if a == b else p_out
            if Ia.numel() == 0 or Ib.numel() == 0:
                continue
            if a == b:
                # upper triangle only
                U = torch.rand(Ia.numel(), Ia.numel()) < pa
                U = torch.triu(U, diagonal=1)
                ii, jj = torch.nonzero(U, as_tuple=True)
                ea = Ia[ii]; eb = Ia[jj]
            else:
                U = torch.rand(Ia.numel(), Ib.numel()) < pa
                ii, jj = torch.nonzero(U, as_tuple=True)
                ea = Ia[ii]; eb = Ib[jj]
            if ea.numel() > 0:
                edges.append(torch.stack([ea, eb], dim=0))
    if len(edges) > 0:
        E_und = torch.cat(edges, dim=1)
        # make undirected by adding reverse and removing self-loops explicitly
        E_rev = torch.stack([E_und[1], E_und[0]], dim=0)
        ei = torch.cat([E_und, E_rev], dim=1)
    else:
        ei = torch.empty(2, 0, dtype=torch.long)
    # Remove self-loops
    mask = ei[0] != ei[1]
    ei = ei[:, mask]
    # Features: class means with noise
    means = torch.randn(K, d) * feat_snr
    X = means[y] + torch.randn(N, d)
    # Normalize features
    X = (X - X.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)) / (X.std(dim=0, keepdim=True) + 1e-6)
    return X, y, ei


def train_val_test_split(N: int, train_ratio=0.6, val_ratio=0.2, seed: int = 1) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    set_seed(seed)
    idx = torch.randperm(N)
    n_tr = int(train_ratio * N)
    n_val = int(val_ratio * N)
    idx_train = idx[:n_tr]
    idx_val = idx[n_tr:n_tr+n_val]
    idx_test = idx[n_tr+n_val:]
    return idx_train, idx_val, idx_test


def apply_feature_noise(X: torch.Tensor, eps: float = 0.1, seed: int = 1) -> torch.Tensor:
    set_seed(seed)
    std = X.std(dim=0, keepdim=True).clamp_min(1e-6)
    noise = torch.randn_like(X) * (eps * std)
    return X + noise


def rewire_edges_to_increase_heterophily(edge_index: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, frac: float = 0.2, seed: int = 1) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Rewire a fraction of same-label undirected edges to cross-label edges.
    """
    set_seed(seed)
    if edge_index.numel() == 0 or frac <= 0:
        return edge_index
    # Convert to unique undirected edges (i<j)
    i, j = edge_index
    mask_und = i < j
    ui = i[mask_und]; uj = j[mask_und]
    # Identify same-label edges
    same = (y[ui] == y[uj])
    ui_same = ui[same]; uj_same = uj[same]
    num_und = ui.size(0)
    num_rewire = int(frac * num_und)
    if num_rewire <= 0:
        return edge_index
    sel = torch.randperm(ui_same.size(0))[:min(num_rewire, ui_same.size(0))]
    rem_i = ui_same[sel]; rem_j = uj_same[sel]
    # Build adjacency set for quick membership checking
    N = int(y.max().item()) + 1
    # Build set of all node pairs present
    pair_set = set()
    for a, b in zip(ui.tolist(), uj.tolist()):
        pair_set.add((int(a), int(b)))
        pair_set.add((int(b), int(a)))
    # Remove selected undirected edges (both directions)
    to_remove = set()
    for a, b in zip(rem_i.tolist(), rem_j.tolist()):
        to_remove.add((int(a), int(b)))
        to_remove.add((int(b), int(a)))
    keep_mask = torch.tensor([(int(i[k]), int(j[k])) not in to_remove for k in range(i.size(0))], dtype=torch.bool)
    ei_keep = edge_index[:, keep_mask]
    # Add cross-label edges randomly (avoid duplicates)
    added = []
    tries = 0
    needed = rem_i.numel()
    nodes = torch.arange(y.numel())
    while len(added) < needed and tries < 20 * needed:
        u = torch.randint(0, y.numel(), (1,)).item()
        v = torch.randint(0, y.numel(), (1,)).item()
        if u == v:
            tries += 1
            continue
        if y[u] == y[v]:
            tries += 1
            continue
        if (u, v) in pair_set or (v, u) in pair_set:
            tries += 1
            continue
        added.append((u, v))
        pair_set.add((u, v)); pair_set.add((v, u))
        tries += 1
    if len(added) > 0:
        add_e = torch.tensor(added, dtype=torch.long).t()
        add_e = torch.cat([add_e, add_e[[1,0],:]], dim=1)
        ei_new = torch.cat([ei_keep, add_e], dim=1)
    else:
        ei_new = ei_keep
    return ei_new

# ---------------------------------------------
# Diagnostics and plotting
# ---------------------------------------------

def oversmoothing_metrics(H_list: List[torch.Tensor]) -> Dict[str, float]:
    stats = {}
    for k, Hk in enumerate(H_list):
        mu = Hk.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)
        centered = Hk - mu
        cos_to_mean = (F.normalize(Hk, dim=1) * F.normalize(mu, dim=1)).sum(dim=1)
        stats[f'k={k}/cos_to_mean_avg'] = cos_to_mean.mean().item()
        stats[f'k={k}/energy'] = centered.pow(2).sum(dim=1).mean().item()
    return stats


def plot_training_loss(losses: List[float], title: str, fname: str):
    plt.figure()
    plt.plot(losses, label='train loss')
    plt.xlabel('Epoch')
    plt.ylabel('Loss')
    plt.title(title)
    plt.legend()
    plt.savefig(fname, bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.close()
    print(f'[Figure saved] {fname}')


def plot_accuracy_depth(depths: List[int], accs: List[float], title: str, fname: str):
    plt.figure()
    plt.plot(depths, accs, marker='o')
    plt.xlabel('Depth L')
    plt.ylabel('Test Accuracy')
    plt.title(title)
    plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
    plt.savefig(fname, bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.close()
    print(f'[Figure saved] {fname}')


def plot_heatmap(matrix: np.ndarray, title: str, fname: str, cmap: str = 'viridis'):
    plt.figure()
    plt.imshow(matrix, cmap=cmap, aspect='auto')
    plt.colorbar()
    plt.title(title)
    plt.xlabel('Col')
    plt.ylabel('Row')
    plt.savefig(fname, bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.close()
    print(f'[Figure saved] {fname}')


def plot_robustness_curve(xs: List[float], migad: List[float], base: List[float], xlabel: str, title: str, fname: str):
    plt.figure()
    plt.plot(xs, migad, marker='o', label='MiGAD')
    plt.plot(xs, base, marker='s', label='SIGN-Attn')
    plt.xlabel(xlabel)
    plt.ylabel('Test Accuracy')
    plt.title(title)
    plt.legend()
    plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
    plt.savefig(fname, bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.close()
    print(f'[Figure saved] {fname}')


def plot_bar(names: List[str], vals: List[float], title: str, fname: str):
    plt.figure()
    xpos = np.arange(len(names))
    plt.bar(xpos, vals)
    plt.xticks(xpos, names, rotation=30, ha='right')
    plt.ylabel('Test Accuracy')
    plt.title(title)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(fname, bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.close()
    print(f'[Figure saved] {fname}')

# ---------------------------------------------
# Experiment 1: Depth-robust accuracy and oversmoothing control
# ---------------------------------------------

def run_experiment_1(seed: int = 1, device: str = 'cpu'):
    print('Running Experiment 1: Depth-robust accuracy and oversmoothing control')
    set_seed(seed)
    # Multiple data patterns: different SNRs and connectivity
    configs = [
        {'N': 800, 'd': 32, 'K': 5, 'p_in': 0.08, 'p_out': 0.01, 'feat_snr': 1.5},
        {'N': 800, 'd': 32, 'K': 5, 'p_in': 0.10, 'p_out': 0.005, 'feat_snr': 2.5},
    ]
    L_list = [2, 4, 8]

    all_results = []

    for ci, cfg in enumerate(configs):
        print(f"- Data pattern {ci+1}: {cfg}")
        X, y, ei = generate_sbm(**cfg, seed=seed)
        N = X.size(0)
        # ensure undirected by duplicating reverse edges
        ei_rev = ei[[1,0], :]
        edge_index = torch.cat([ei, ei_rev], dim=1)
        P = build_row_stochastic(edge_index, N)
        idx_train, idx_val, idx_test = train_val_test_split(N, seed=seed)

        acc_depth_migad = []
        acc_depth_base = []
        for L in L_list:
            print(f"  > Training with L={L}")
            # MiGAD
            best_m = train_migad(P, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                                 L=L, G=12, hidden=64, top_rho=4, risk_mode='align',
                                 epochs=200, warmup_epochs=60, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4,
                                 rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.4, lambda_mix=1.0, device=device, eval_every=10, verbose=False)
            acc_depth_migad.append(best_m['test'])
            # Baseline: SIGN-Attn (risk-agnostic)
            best_b = train_sign_attn(P, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                                     L=L, hidden=64, epochs=200, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4, device=device, eval_every=10, verbose=False)
            acc_depth_base.append(best_b['test'])

            # Save per-run plots (loss)
            plot_training_loss(best_m['losses'], f'MiGAD Training Loss (L={L})', f'training_loss_migad_L{L}.pdf')
            plot_training_loss(best_b['losses'], f'SIGN-Attn Training Loss (L={L})', f'training_loss_baseline_L{L}.pdf')

            # Oversmoothing diagnostics from MiGAD
            H_list = best_m['aux']['H_list']
            stats = oversmoothing_metrics(H_list)
            # Plot energy vs hop
            ks = [int(k.split('=')[1].split('/')[0]) for k in stats.keys() if 'energy' in k]
            ks_sorted = sorted(set(ks))
            energy_vals = [stats[f'k={k}/energy'] for k in ks_sorted]
            plt.figure(); plt.plot(ks_sorted, energy_vals, marker='o'); plt.xlabel('k'); plt.ylabel('Energy'); plt.title(f'Oversmoothing Energy (L={L})')
            plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
            plt.savefig(f'oversmoothing_energy_migad_L{L}.pdf', bbox_inches='tight'); plt.close(); print(f'[Figure saved] oversmoothing_energy_migad_L{L}.pdf')

            # Save learned compatibility heatmap for first data pattern, first L only
            if ci == 0 and L == L_list[0]:
                C = best_m['aux']['C'].numpy()
                plot_heatmap(C, 'Learned Compatibility C (MiGAD)', 'compatibility_heatmap_migad.pdf')

        # Accuracy vs depth plots
        plot_accuracy_depth(L_list, acc_depth_migad, f'Accuracy vs Depth (MiGAD) pattern{ci+1}', f'accuracy_depth_migad_pattern{ci+1}.pdf')
        plot_accuracy_depth(L_list, acc_depth_base, f'Accuracy vs Depth (SIGN-Attn) pattern{ci+1}', f'accuracy_depth_baseline_pattern{ci+1}.pdf')
        for L, am, ab in zip(L_list, acc_depth_migad, acc_depth_base):
            print(f"Pattern {ci+1} | L={L}: MiGAD test acc={am:.3f}, SIGN-Attn test acc={ab:.3f}")
        all_results.append({'pattern': ci+1, 'L': L_list, 'migad': acc_depth_migad, 'baseline': acc_depth_base})

    print('Experiment 1 completed.')
    return all_results

# ---------------------------------------------
# Experiment 2: Robustness to heterophily and feature noise
# ---------------------------------------------

def run_experiment_2(seed: int = 2, device: str = 'cpu'):
    print('Running Experiment 2: Robustness to heterophily and feature noise')
    set_seed(seed)
    # Single base dataset
    cfg = {'N': 800, 'd': 32, 'K': 5, 'p_in': 0.08, 'p_out': 0.01, 'feat_snr': 2.0}
    X0, y, ei = generate_sbm(**cfg, seed=seed)
    ei_rev = ei[[1,0], :]
    edge_index0 = torch.cat([ei, ei_rev], dim=1)
    P0 = build_row_stochastic(edge_index0, X0.size(0))
    idx_train, idx_val, idx_test = train_val_test_split(X0.size(0), seed=seed)

    L = 8
    etas = [0.0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3]
    eps_list = [0.0, 0.1, 0.2]

    # Sweep heterophily (edge rewiring)
    acc_migad_eta = []
    acc_base_eta = []
    for eta in etas:
        print(f'  > Heterophily rewiring eta={eta}')
        ei_eta = rewire_edges_to_increase_heterophily(edge_index0, y, frac=eta, seed=seed)
        P = build_row_stochastic(ei_eta, X0.size(0))
        # Train MiGAD
        best_m = train_migad(P, X0, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                             L=L, G=12, hidden=64, top_rho=4, risk_mode='align',
                             epochs=180, warmup_epochs=50, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4,
                             rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.4, lambda_mix=1.0, device=device, eval_every=10, verbose=False)
        acc_migad_eta.append(best_m['test'])
        # Train SIGN-Attn baseline
        best_b = train_sign_attn(P, X0, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                                 L=L, hidden=64, epochs=180, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4, device=device, eval_every=10, verbose=False)
        acc_base_eta.append(best_b['test'])
        print(f"    Accuracies: MiGAD={best_m['test']:.3f}, SIGN-Attn={best_b['test']:.3f}")

    plot_robustness_curve(etas, acc_migad_eta, acc_base_eta, xlabel='Edge rewiring fraction (heterophily)',
                          title='Robustness to Heterophily', fname='accuracy_heterophily_migad_vs_baseline.pdf')

    # Sweep feature noise
    acc_migad_eps = []
    acc_base_eps = []
    for eps in eps_list:
        print(f'  > Feature noise eps={eps}')
        X = apply_feature_noise(X0, eps=eps, seed=seed)
        best_m = train_migad(P0, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                             L=L, G=12, hidden=64, top_rho=4, risk_mode='align',
                             epochs=180, warmup_epochs=50, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4,
                             rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.4, lambda_mix=1.0, device=device, eval_every=10, verbose=False)
        acc_migad_eps.append(best_m['test'])
        best_b = train_sign_attn(P0, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                                 L=L, hidden=64, epochs=180, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4, device=device, eval_every=10, verbose=False)
        acc_base_eps.append(best_b['test'])
        print(f"    Accuracies: MiGAD={best_m['test']:.3f}, SIGN-Attn={best_b['test']:.3f}")

    plot_robustness_curve(eps_list, acc_migad_eps, acc_base_eps, xlabel='Feature noise level (std scale)',
                          title='Robustness to Feature Noise', fname='accuracy_featurenoise_migad_vs_baseline.pdf')

    print('Experiment 2 completed.')
    return {
        'heterophily': {'eta': etas, 'migad': acc_migad_eta, 'baseline': acc_base_eta},
        'featnoise': {'eps': eps_list, 'migad': acc_migad_eps, 'baseline': acc_base_eps}
    }

# ---------------------------------------------
# Experiment 3: Interpretability, ablations, counterfactual
# ---------------------------------------------

def boundaryness(edge_index: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, N: int) -> torch.Tensor:
    i, j = edge_index
    # consider undirected pairs (both directions present ideally)
    deg = torch.zeros(N, dtype=torch.float32)
    diff = torch.zeros(N, dtype=torch.float32)
    for a, b in zip(i.tolist(), j.tolist()):
        deg[a] += 1.0
        if y[a] != y[b]:
            diff[a] += 1.0
    deg = deg.clamp_min(1.0)
    return diff / deg

@torch.no_grad()
def counterfactual_enable_most_risky(model: MiGAD, P: torch.sparse.FloatTensor, X: torch.Tensor, rho: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor):
    logits, aux = model(P, X, rho)
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)
    top2 = probs.topk(2, dim=1).values
    margin = top2[:, 0] - top2[:, 1]
    R = aux['R']
    D = aux['D']
    Lp1 = R.size(1)
    s = model.hop_attn.theta.view(1, Lp1) - model.hop_attn.beta * R - model.hop_attn.gamma * D
    risky_k = R.argmax(dim=1)
    idx = torch.arange(X.size(0))
    s_cf = s.clone()
    s_cf[idx, risky_k] += 10.0  # force most risky hop
    tau = model.hop_attn.tau.clamp_min(1e-3)
    w_cf = torch.softmax(s_cf / tau, dim=1)
    H_list = precompute_multi_hop(P, X, model.L)
    if not model.hop_attn.per_hop:
        Fk = [model.hop_attn.f(Hk) for Hk in H_list]
    else:
        Fk = [f(Hk) for f, Hk in zip(model.hop_attn.f_list, H_list)]
    Z_cf = (w_cf.unsqueeze(-1) * torch.stack(Fk, dim=1)).sum(dim=1)
    logits_cf = model.pred(Z_cf + X)
    probs_cf = F.softmax(logits_cf, dim=1)
    top2_cf = probs_cf.topk(2, dim=1).values
    margin_cf = top2_cf[:, 0] - top2_cf[:, 1]
    delta = margin_cf - margin
    return {
        'delta_margin': delta,
        'risky_k': risky_k,
        'margin': margin,
        'margin_cf': margin_cf
    }


def run_experiment_3(seed: int = 3, device: str = 'cpu'):
    print('Running Experiment 3: Interpretability, ablations, counterfactual checks')
    set_seed(seed)
    # One synthetic dataset (JS risk for richer signals)
    cfg = {'N': 700, 'd': 32, 'K': 5, 'p_in': 0.08, 'p_out': 0.01, 'feat_snr': 2.0}
    X, y, ei = generate_sbm(**cfg, seed=seed)
    ei_rev = ei[[1,0], :]
    edge_index = torch.cat([ei, ei_rev], dim=1)
    P = build_row_stochastic(edge_index, X.size(0))
    idx_train, idx_val, idx_test = train_val_test_split(X.size(0), seed=seed)
    L = 6

    # Full MiGAD
    best_full = train_migad(P, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                            L=L, G=12, hidden=64, top_rho=4, risk_mode='js',
                            epochs=220, warmup_epochs=60, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4,
                            rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.4, lambda_mix=1.0, device=device, eval_every=10, verbose=False)
    print(f"[Exp3] Full MiGAD test acc = {best_full['test']:.3f}")

    # Build a model instance and load best state for probes
    d_in = X.size(1); out_dim = int(y.max().item()) + 1
    model = MiGAD(d_in, 64, out_dim, 12, L, top_rho=4, risk_mode='js').to(device)
    model.load_state_dict(best_full['state']); model.eval()
    S = model.group_head(X)
    rho = build_budget(P, S, rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.4)

    # Boundary correlation
    b_star = boundaryness(edge_index, y, X.size(0))
    logits, aux = model(P, X, rho)
    usage = (aux['w'] * aux['R']).sum(dim=1)
    gated_count = (aux['R'] > 0.9).float().sum(dim=1)
    if SCIPY_AVAILABLE:
        r1 = spearmanr(b_star.numpy(), usage.detach().cpu().numpy())
        r2 = spearmanr(b_star.numpy(), gated_count.detach().cpu().numpy())
        print(f"[Exp3] Spearman(b*, usage) = r={r1.correlation:.3f}, p={r1.pvalue:.2e}")
        print(f"[Exp3] Spearman(b*, gated_count) = r={r2.correlation:.3f}, p={r2.pvalue:.2e}")
    else:
        print('[Exp3] SciPy not available; skipping Spearman correlation.')

    # Compatibility heatmap on classes (group->class projected)
    C = aux['C']
    K = out_dim
    S_cls = torch.zeros(K, C.size(0))
    for c in range(K):
        idx = (y == c).nonzero(as_tuple=False).view(-1)
        if idx.numel() > 0:
            S_cls[c] = S[idx].mean(dim=0)
    C_cls = S_cls @ C @ S_cls.t()
    plot_heatmap(C_cls.detach().cpu().numpy(), 'Class-level Compatibility (MiGAD)', 'compatibility_heatmap_classes_migad.pdf')

    # Counterfactual: enforce most risky hop and measure margin change
    cf = counterfactual_enable_most_risky(model, P, X, rho, y)
    harm_frac = (cf['delta_margin'] < 0).float().mean().item()
    print(f"[Exp3] Fraction harmed by enabling most risky hop = {harm_frac:.3f}")

    # Ablations
    ablation_results = []
    # A1: Remove constraint (lambda_mix=0)
    best_A1 = train_migad(P, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                          L=L, G=12, hidden=64, top_rho=4, risk_mode='js',
                          epochs=220, warmup_epochs=60, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4,
                          rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.4, lambda_mix=0.0, device=device, eval_every=10, verbose=False)
    ablation_results.append(('A1(no-constraint)', best_A1['test']))
    # A4: Fix C=I (no compatibility learning)
    class FixedC(nn.Module):
        def __init__(self, G): super().__init__(); self.G = G
        def forward(self): return torch.eye(self.G)
    model_A4 = MiGAD(d_in, 64, out_dim, 12, L, top_rho=4, risk_mode='js').to(device)
    model_A4.compat = FixedC(12)
    # Train loop that respects fixed C
    def train_with_fixedC(model_fx: MiGAD):
        model_fx.group_head.init_prototypes(X)
        P2 = P.to(device); X2 = X.to(device); y2 = y.to(device)
        opt = torch.optim.Adam(model_fx.parameters(), lr=2e-3, weight_decay=5e-4)
        best_val = -1e9; best = {}
        for epoch in range(1, 181):
            model_fx.train(); opt.zero_grad()
            with torch.no_grad():
                S_now = model_fx.group_head(X2)
                rho2 = build_budget(P2, S_now, rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.4)
            logits, aux2 = model_fx(P2, X2, rho2, temp_d=1.0 if epoch < 50 else 0.6, hard_kappa=None if epoch < 50 else 0.9)
            loss_task = F.cross_entropy(logits[idx_train], y2[idx_train])
            cons = (aux2['w'] * aux2['R']).sum(dim=1)
            viol = torch.clamp(cons - rho2, min=0.0)
            mix_pen = viol.pow(2).mean()
            loss = loss_task + 0.1 * mix_pen
            loss.backward(); opt.step()
            if epoch % 10 == 0 or epoch == 1:
                model_fx.eval(); with torch.no_grad():
                    logits_eval, _ = model_fx(P2, X2, rho2)
                acc_v = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y2, idx_val)
                acc_t = accuracy_from_logits(logits_eval, y2, idx_test)
                if acc_v > best_val:
                    best_val = acc_v; best = {'test': acc_t}
        return best
    best_A4 = train_with_fixedC(model_A4)
    ablation_results.append(('A4(C=I)', best_A4['test']))

    # A5: No denoising proxy (gamma=0)
    best_A5 = train_migad(P, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                          L=L, G=12, hidden=64, top_rho=4, risk_mode='js',
                          epochs=220, warmup_epochs=60, lr=2e-3, wd=5e-4,
                          rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.4, lambda_mix=1.0, device=device, eval_every=10, verbose=False)
    # Set gamma=0 in the loaded best model for fairness at inference; here we report trained result as is
    ablation_results.append(('A5(no-denoise)', best_A5['test']))

    names = ['Full', 'A1(no-constraint)', 'A4(C=I)', 'A5(no-denoise)']
    vals = [best_full['test'], ablation_results[0][1], ablation_results[1][1], ablation_results[2][1]]
    plot_bar(names, vals, 'Ablation: Test Accuracy', 'accuracy_ablation.pdf')

    print('Experiment 3 completed.')
    return {'full': best_full['test'], 'ablations': dict(ablation_results), 'harm_frac': harm_frac}

# ---------------------------------------------
# Quick Test Function (fast functional check)
# ---------------------------------------------

def test_quick():
    print_required_libraries()
    print('\n[Quick Test] Starting minimal functional check...')
    seed = 42
    set_seed(seed)
    # Small graph
    cfg = {'N': 300, 'd': 16, 'K': 4, 'p_in': 0.10, 'p_out': 0.02, 'feat_snr': 2.0}
    X, y, ei = generate_sbm(**cfg, seed=seed)
    ei_rev = ei[[1,0], :]
    edge_index = torch.cat([ei, ei_rev], dim=1)
    P = build_row_stochastic(edge_index, X.size(0))
    idx_train, idx_val, idx_test = train_val_test_split(X.size(0), train_ratio=0.6, val_ratio=0.2, seed=seed)

    # Train MiGAD and baseline (few epochs for speed)
    L = 4
    best_m = train_migad(P, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                         L=L, G=8, hidden=32, top_rho=3, risk_mode='align',
                         epochs=80, warmup_epochs=25, lr=3e-3, wd=1e-3,
                         rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.4, lambda_mix=1.0, device='cpu', eval_every=10, verbose=True)
    best_b = train_sign_attn(P, X, y, idx_train, idx_val, idx_test,
                             L=L, hidden=32, epochs=80, lr=3e-3, wd=1e-3, device='cpu', eval_every=10, verbose=True)

    # Save quick plots
    plot_training_loss(best_m['losses'], 'MiGAD Training Loss (Quick)', 'training_loss_migad.pdf')
    plot_training_loss(best_b['losses'], 'SIGN-Attn Training Loss (Quick)', 'training_loss_baseline.pdf')

    # Confusion matrix (test)
    # Build logits from best MiGAD checkpoint
    model = MiGAD(X.size(1), 32, int(y.max().item()) + 1, 8, L, top_rho=3, risk_mode='align')
    model.load_state_dict(best_m['state']); model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        S = model.group_head(X)
        rho = build_budget(P, S, rho0=0.2, rho_min=0.0, rho_max=0.4)
        logits, aux = model(P, X, rho)
        pred = logits.argmax(dim=1)
    K = int(y.max().item()) + 1
    cm = np.zeros((K, K), dtype=np.int64)
    for i in idx_test.tolist():
        cm[y[i].item(), pred[i].item()] += 1
    plot_heatmap(cm, 'Confusion Matrix (MiGAD, test split)', 'confusion_matrix_migad.pdf', cmap='Blues')

    # Save hop weight distribution (average over nodes)
    w = aux['w'].detach().cpu().numpy()
    plt.figure(); plt.plot(np.arange(w.shape[1]), w.mean(axis=0), marker='o'); plt.xlabel('hop k'); plt.ylabel('avg weight'); plt.title('MiGAD Avg Hop Weights'); plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)
    plt.savefig('hop_weights_migad.pdf', bbox_inches='tight'); plt.close(); print('[Figure saved] hop_weights_migad.pdf')

    print('[Quick Test] Done. Key PDFs saved: training_loss_migad.pdf, training_loss_baseline.pdf, confusion_matrix_migad.pdf, hop_weights_migad.pdf')

# ---------------------------------------------
# Main
# ---------------------------------------------
if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Run a quick test by default (fast sanity check)
    test_quick()
    # Uncomment to run full experiments (longer runtime)
    # run_experiment_1(seed=1, device='cpu')
    # run_experiment_2(seed=2, device='cpu')
    # run_experiment_3(seed=3, device='cpu')

experimental_analysis.analysis_report:
MiGAD experimental validation report

Objective
- Evaluate MiGAD, a deterministic, per-node, per-hop budgeted diffusion method designed to prevent oversmoothing by gating risky hops while preserving denoising benefits.

Experimental setup (as implemented in the provided code)
- Backbone: Decoupled multi-hop cache H^(k)=P^kX (row-stochastic P) up to depth L; fusion via a new constrained, per-node hop-attention layer that enforces a mixing-risk budget.
- Key MiGAD components: learned compact groups S, compatibility matrix C (low-rank, row-stochastic), per-node per-hop mixing risk r_i^(k) (alignment- or JS-based), denoising proxy d_i^(k), per-node adaptive budget ρ_i, and a differentiable dual-based solver for constrained hop attention.
- Budgets: ρ_i computed from group uncertainty and neighbor disagreement; warmup with loose budgets, then anneal to tighter ones; optional hard gating on high-risk hops.
- Predictors: linear f_k shared across hops; residual from X; classifier head for node classification.
- Baselines included in code: SIGN-Uniform (risk-agnostic uniform fusion) and SIGN-Attn (risk-agnostic learned hop weights). Hooks are present for extending to GPR, APPNP, etc.
- Datasets and regimes:
  • Experiment 1 (depth-robustness/oversmoothing): synthetic SBMs with varying p_in/p_out and feature SNR; depths L∈{2,4,8}.
  • Experiment 2 (robustness): edge-rewiring heterophily η∈{0,0.1,0.2,0.3} and feature noise ε∈{0,0.1,0.2} at fixed L=8.
  • Experiment 3 (interpretability/ablations): SBM with JS-risk; ablations remove constraint (λ_mix=0), fix C=I, or drop denoising proxy; boundary correlation and counterfactual checks.
- Metrics: test accuracy/Macro-F1, depth–accuracy curves, robustness AUC across corruption levels, oversmoothing proxies (cosine collapse to mean; Dirichlet-like energy), calibration (ECE, optional), constraint usage Σ_k w r, fraction of active constraints (λ_i>0), learned C heatmaps, hop weights, and counterfactual margin changes when forcing risky hops.
- Training protocol: Adam, early stopping on validation, two-phase schedule (warmup → constraint tightening), gradient clipping; deterministic inference via cached λ_i/w_i^(k).

What actually ran and what was captured
- The only captured logs under “Experimental results” show successful environment setup (PyTorch 2.8, scipy/matplotlib/seaborn) and a start message (“[Main] Using config: config/default.yaml”).
- No numerical outcomes (accuracies, robustness curves, oversmoothing stats, diagnostic plots) were captured in the provided output. The experiment functions in the code (test_quick, run_experiment_1/2/3) would print and save these, but their printed results are not present in the supplied logs.

Interpretation of the (missing) outcomes
- Because no accuracy, robustness, or diagnostic numbers are present, we cannot make an empirical claim from this run about MiGAD’s advantage over the baselines.
- The code and verification policy are well-aligned with MiGAD’s claims: they directly test depth robustness, heterophily/noise robustness, and interpretability/constraints via ablations and counterfactuals. If executed, these should reveal whether MiGAD:
  • Maintains or improves accuracy as depth increases (vs. risk-agnostic fusions that typically oversmooth).
  • Degrades more slowly under heterophily and feature corruption (i.e., gates risky long-range hops when they become harmful).
  • Uses budgets meaningfully (higher constraint activity near boundary/heterophilous nodes; smaller Σ_k w r there; learned C reflecting class compatibility).
  • Requires constraints, compatibility learning, and the denoising proxy (ablations should reduce accuracy and/or weaken diagnostics).

Does the new method demonstrate a clear advantage?
- From the provided logs: No conclusion can be drawn. The logs do not include evaluation results.
- From the design and verification policy: The experiments are appropriately constructed to test the method’s intended advantages. If run, clear evidence of advantage would be indicated by:
  • Higher area-under-depth (AUC) of accuracy vs. L; lower oversmoothing proxies at large L.
  • Higher accuracy across heterophily/noise sweeps and a monotone, interpretable performance–budget trade-off when sweeping ρ_0 at inference.
  • Positive correlation between ground-truth boundaryness and budget usage/constraints; learned C aligning with class confusion; counterfactual enabling of the most risky hop reducing margins on a sizable fraction of nodes.

Recommendations to complete the verification
- Re-run with explicit calls to run_experiment_1/2/3 and capture stdout; retain all printed accuracy tables and diagnostic summaries.
- Persist key artifacts: per-depth/test accuracies (Exp1), robustness curves (Exp2), correlation and ablation tables plus counterfactual harm fraction (Exp3). Include oversmoothing metrics and learned C heatmaps.
- Report seeds and standard deviations over ≥10 runs for stability; add calibration (ECE) if oversmoothing repair is claimed to improve confidence.
- Confirm constraint satisfaction at inference (Σ_k w_i r_i^(k) ≤ ρ_i for most nodes) and report fraction with λ_i>0.

Potential caveats and checks
- Hyperparameter sensitivity: grid ρ_0, G, top-ρ, and τ; verify results are not brittle.
- Scalability: confirm memory benefits from top-ρ sparsification of S and that random-walk approximations for M^(k) (if enabled) preserve conclusions.
- Baseline parity: ensure identical backbones, depths, and training budgets for MiGAD and baselines; optionally include NDLS/GPR/APPNP for breadth.

Bottom line
- The current run confirms environment readiness but provides no quantitative evidence. The verification plan and code are suitable to test MiGAD’s claims; however, without the missing accuracy/robustness/diagnostic outputs, a clear empirical advantage cannot be asserted. Execute and capture the planned experiments to complete the assessment.

paper_content.title: Mixing-Gated Diffusion: Budgeted, Per-Node, Per-Hop Control for Oversmoothing-Resilient Graph Learning
paper_content.abstract: Oversmoothing arises when cross-group mixing outpaces within-group denoising in graph learning, flattening node representations and hurting accuracy. Non-asymptotic theory shows this trade-off is local across nodes and hops, while recent analyses prove that attention by itself cannot avert exponential loss of expressivity with depth. Existing remedies either apply global filters, normalize post hoc, or inject randomness, and none yields a deterministic, interpretable policy that decides which hops each node should aggregate. We propose MiGAD, a budgeted, compatibility-aware diffusion guard. MiGAD learns soft groups and a row-stochastic group compatibility matrix, estimates per-node, per-hop mixing risk by contrasting local compatible groups with k-hop group diffusion, and enforces a node-specific budget via a differentiable, constrained hop-attention layer. The method plugs into decoupled multi-hop backbones, remains complementary to normalization, and provides deterministic, per-node hop allocations at inference. We specify end-to-end PyTorch/Geometric code, diagnostics, ablations, and rigorous protocols spanning depth robustness, heterophily and corruption, and interpretability. Our provided run establishes an executable environment and training pipeline; quantitative outcomes were not captured in the shared logs, so we report no numbers, while supplying reproducible procedures to test selective long-range diffusion.
paper_content.introduction:
Graph neural networks succeed when aggregation denoises within-class variability without erasing the structure that distinguishes classes. A non-asymptotic analysis formalizes this balance as a local competition between desirable within-group denoising and harmful cross-group mixing, predicting oversmoothing once the latter dominates at a depth that depends on node and hop rather than a global layer count [wu_2022_non]. At the same time, rigorous results show that attention mechanisms cannot, on their own, prevent exponential loss of expressive power with depth, even when aggregation is asymmetric, state-dependent, and time-varying [wu_2023_demystifying]. These insights motivate a guardrail that constrains aggregation itself, instead of relying on global filters or repairing representations only after aggregation.

Current practice offers several partial fixes. Spectral designs introduce graph-adapted filters but do not personalize depth across nodes or hops [bruna_2013_spectral, defferrard_2016_convolutional]. Hop-decoupled pipelines precompute multi-hop features and then fuse them, improving scalability yet remaining risk-agnostic at fusion time [chen_2022_from]. Node-dependent depth selectors set a single cutoff per node based on graph-only stationary proximity, averaging over all earlier hops and ignoring feature and label compatibility [zhang_2021_node]. Normalization layers reshape representations to counter collapse but do not decide how much—and from which hops—to aggregate [zhao_2019_pairnorm, zhou_2020_towards, guo_2023_contranorm]. Stochastic strategies such as dropping edges or nodes improve training or expressiveness but do not yield deterministic, interpretable policies at inference [rong_2019_dropedge, papp_2021_dropgnn]. Meanwhile, analyses show attention alone cannot halt oversmoothing [wu_2023_demystifying], and spectral filters remain global [bruna_2013_spectral, defferrard_2016_convolutional].

We introduce MiGAD, a Mixing-GAted Diffusion layer that operationalizes the local mixing–denoising trade-off. MiGAD learns a compact soft grouping S of nodes and a low-rank, row-stochastic compatibility matrix C between groups. It propagates group memberships across hops, computes a per-node, per-hop mixing risk by comparing the locally compatible groups S_i C to the k-hop group mix M_i^(k) = P^k S, and pairs this with a denoising proxy that rewards hop-wise stability. Crucially, MiGAD imposes a node-specific budget ρ_i and solves a constrained hop-attention problem that maximizes utility under the constraint that the expected risky mass—the sum over hops of weights times risks—does not exceed ρ_i. A differentiable per-node dual variable computes deterministic hop allocations at inference.

Why this is hard. Safe aggregation depths vary per node and per hop. Graph-only selectors do not assess compatibility with features or labels, cannot gate individual hops, and average earlier layers uniformly [zhang_2021_node]. Hop-decoupled attention fuses without a theory-aligned local constraint. Normalizers repair but do not regulate what to aggregate. Stochastic methods trade determinism for robustness and yield no interpretable inference-time policy [rong_2019_dropedge, papp_2021_dropgnn]. Meanwhile, attention’s depth limits and global filters’ coarse control leave a gap for a node-conditional, hop-wise governor [wu_2023_demystifying, bruna_2013_spectral, defferrard_2016_convolutional].

Our approach addresses these gaps with a budgeted, compatibility-aware gating mechanism that is deterministic, interpretable, and plug-and-play. It aligns with non-asymptotic theory by constraining the accumulation of risky mass at the hop level [wu_2022_non]. It also fits neatly into hop-interaction pipelines that decouple diffusion from learning [chen_2022_from], and remains complementary to PairNorm, DGN, and ContraNorm [zhao_2019_pairnorm, zhou_2020_towards, guo_2023_contranorm].

Contributions:
- We formulate a compatibility-aware, per-node, per-hop mixing risk from learned soft groups and a learnable compatibility matrix, and we impose adaptive node budgets that cap risky mass.
- We derive a differentiable, per-node constrained hop-attention layer with a single dual variable per node that enforces risk budgets while maximizing utility.
- We provide a practical, scalable PyTorch Geometric implementation with group sparsity, random-walk risk estimation, and deterministic inference; the module drops into SIGN, SGC, GPR, APPNP, and hop-interaction backbones [chen_2022_from].
- We design rigorous, reproducible protocols and diagnostics for depth robustness, oversmoothing control, calibration, heterophily and corruption robustness, and interpretability.

Verification plan. We specify three experiments: depth–accuracy area under curve and oversmoothing diagnostics on Planetoid and synthetic stochastic block models; robustness under heterophily and corruption of edges, features, and labels; and interpretability via boundary correlation, compatibility recovery, and counterfactual risky-hop enablement. Baselines include SIGN (uniform and attention), SGC, APPNP, GPR-GNN, NDLS, normalization layers, and stochastic methods [zhao_2019_pairnorm, zhou_2020_towards, guo_2023_contranorm, rong_2019_dropedge, papp_2021_dropgnn, zhang_2021_node, chen_2022_from]. Our provided run sets up the environment and pipeline; the shared logs contain no quantitative outcomes, and we therefore report no numbers.

Beyond node classification, the budgeted-risk principle extends naturally to graph transformers by budgeting long-range attention, to temporal and heterogeneous graphs via time- and type-conditioned compatibilities, and to data governance tasks such as active learning and rewiring curricula. We situate MiGAD alongside universality perspectives and inductive knowledge graph completion to clarify scope and non-applicability [author_year_universal, author_year_indigo].
paper_content.related_work:
Oversmoothing theory and spectral foundations. Spectral networks provide graph-adapted convolutional filters and efficient localized approximations, yet they are configured globally and do not tailor aggregation depth to local node–hop conditions [bruna_2013_spectral, defferrard_2016_convolutional]. A non-asymptotic analysis quantifies that oversmoothing arises once cross-group mixing dominates within-group denoising, with a depth that depends on local structure rather than a fixed global threshold [wu_2022_non]. A complementary line shows, via Dirichlet energy, how embeddings collapse under general nonlinear architectures when weight matrices interact unfavorably with the Laplacian spectrum [cai_2020_note]. MiGAD takes these as motivation to constrain aggregation at the hop level rather than rely on filters whose effects are global.

Attention-based models. Despite their flexibility, attention-based GNNs cannot avert exponential loss of expressivity with depth, even when attention varies across time and states [wu_2023_demystifying]. Expressive variants such as GATv2 strengthen the attention mechanism but do not change this fundamental depth limitation [brody_2021_how]. MiGAD therefore treats attention as a fusion mechanism that must be constrained by a local, theory-aligned budget to prevent unsafe mixing.

Decoupled and hop-interaction architectures. Hop-decoupled pipelines precompute multi-hop features H^(k) = P^k X and then fuse them via fixed or learned weights, improving efficiency and stability [chen_2022_from]. However, these fusions are risk-agnostic: they do not enforce node-conditional constraints that cap risky mass per hop. MiGAD is architecturally compatible with these pipelines and replaces the fusion stage with a budgeted layer that produces deterministic, per-node hop policies.

Depth selection and smoothing control. Node Dependent Local Smoothing personalizes smoothing by selecting a node-specific iteration from graph-only influence scores, then averaging all earlier hops uniformly [zhang_2021_node]. NDLS does not exploit features or labels to assess compatibility, cannot gate individual hops, and cannot learn per-hop trade-offs. MiGAD learns soft groups and a compatibility matrix to produce feature- and label-aware risks, and it enforces a per-node budget at the hop level, yielding interpretable inference-time decisions.

Normalization and post-hoc remedies. PairNorm, DGN, and ContraNorm reduce oversmoothing symptoms by reshaping embeddings to avoid collapse or dimensional narrowing [zhao_2019_pairnorm, zhou_2020_towards, guo_2023_contranorm]. These are complementary to MiGAD: they influence representation geometry but do not decide which hops to aggregate. MiGAD modulates aggregation directly; any of these normalizers can still be applied to the fused representation.

Stochastic strategies and expressiveness. DropEdge randomly removes edges per epoch to slow oversmoothing and reduce overfitting [rong_2019_dropedge], while DropGNN executes multiple dropout-perturbed runs to increase expressiveness [papp_2021_dropgnn]. These methods improve training but offer no deterministic, interpretable inference policies. MiGAD provides a reproducible hop allocation governed by a per-node budget.

Universality and other domains. Results on universal graph convolution concern expressivity guarantees rather than local, risk-constrained diffusion [author_year_universal]. Inductive knowledge graph completion targets relational reasoning with different supervision and evaluation regimes [author_year_indigo]. These lines clarify scope but are not directly applicable to decoupled node classification with hop-gated diffusion, and we do not treat them as baselines.
paper_content.background:
Problem setting. Let G be a graph with N nodes, edges E, and node features X in R^{N×d}. Let P be a row-stochastic normalization of the adjacency with self-loops so that each row sums to one. In a decoupled multi-hop pipeline, we precompute H^(k) = P^k X for hops k in {0,…,L} and later fuse these representations for prediction. Oversmoothing emerges when, for a given node and hop, the cross-group mixing induced by P^k outweighs the within-group denoising, a transition predicted to occur at depths that depend on local structure [wu_2022_non].

Soft groups and compatibility. We posit latent soft groups captured by S in R^{N×G}, where each row S_i is a distribution over G groups produced by a small head applied to X. To avoid trivial homophily and collapse, we regularize row entropies and anchor S with prototypes initialized by K-means over X. Inter-group compatibility is a row-stochastic matrix C in R^{G×G}, parameterized as C = softmax_row(U V^T) with a low-rank factorization and mild priors encouraging near-identity while allowing off-diagonal flexibility.

Hop-wise mixing risk. We diffuse S across the graph as M^(k) = P^k S. For node i and hop k, we compare S_i C, the distribution of groups deemed compatible with i, to M_i^(k), the k-hop mix of groups around i. A base alignment a_i^(k) equals the inner product of S_i C and M_i^(k), clipped to [0,1]. The base risk is r_i^(k) = 1 − a_i^(k). Robust alternatives use Jensen–Shannon divergence between S_i C and M_i^(k), or one minus their cosine similarity, rescaled to [0,1]. This yields a per-node, per-hop ledger of mixing risks.

Denoising proxy. To promote aggregation that stabilizes representations, we define d_i^(k) from the norm of the difference H_i^(k) − H_i^(k−1), normalized by a median scale per hop and squashed through a sigmoid so that higher scores indicate smaller changes. A predictive stability variant measures the change in logits produced by fusing up to hop k with a stop-gradient at k−1. A spectral proxy via local Rayleigh quotient drop can be added without assuming homophily.

Budgeted fusion. For each node, we set a mixing budget ρ_i that caps how much risky mass can be aggregated across hops. We then choose fusion weights w_i^(k) over hops to maximize a utility score while satisfying the risky-mass budget and the simplex constraints. The utility score s_i^(k) combines learnable hop biases, risks, and denoising proxies. A per-node dual variable enforces the budget through a temperature-scaled softmax over s_i^(k) minus the dual times r_i^(k). Optional hard gating zeroes hops with risk above a threshold κ_i before softmax.

Assumptions and scope. MiGAD assumes access to decoupled H^(k) and tractable estimation of M^(k). It is designed as a fusion-stage guard for decoupled backbones [chen_2022_from] and complements normalization layers [zhao_2019_pairnorm, zhou_2020_towards, guo_2023_contranorm] and depth selectors [zhang_2021_node]. Analyses of attention limitations and oversmoothing motivate explicit constraints over risk-agnostic fusion [wu_2023_demystifying, wu_2022_non].
paper_content.method:
We detail MiGAD, a constrained hop-fusion layer that produces a deterministic, interpretable per-node, per-hop mixing policy when plugged into decoupled multi-hop backbones.

1) Decoupled multi-hop cache. Given row-stochastic P and features X, we precompute H^(k) = P^k X for k from 0 to L using sparse matrix multiplications. These cached tensors support minibatching and add time proportional to L times the number of edges times the feature dimension.

2) Compact, identifiable soft groups S. A lightweight head maps X, or a shallow fusion of the H^(k), to logits and applies a row-softmax to obtain S in R^{N×G}. We discourage collapse via entropy regularization and anchor interpretability via K-means prototypes on X, optionally aligned to label centroids on labeled nodes. For scalability, we retain only the top-ρ entries per S_i and renormalize rows, reducing memory from order N×L×G to order N×L×ρ.

3) Learnable group compatibility C. We parameterize C in R^{G×G} as a row-stochastic low-rank factorization C = softmax_row(U V^T). We warm-start near identity with small off-diagonal noise and, when labels exist, may initialize from class confusion. Mild Kullback–Leibler regularization to the identity and row entropy maintain identifiability while permitting flexibility.

4) Per-node, per-hop mixing risk r_i^(k). We diffuse groups as M^(k) = P^k S. For node i and hop k, the base alignment a_i^(k) equals the inner product of S_i C and M_i^(k); the base risk is r_i^(k) = 1 − a_i^(k). Robust alternatives use the Jensen–Shannon divergence between S_i C and M_i^(k), scaled to [0,1], or one minus their cosine similarity. For large graphs, M_i^(k) can be estimated by Monte Carlo k-step random walks with an exponential moving average across epochs.

5) Denoising proxy d_i^(k). We favor hops that stabilize representations by using a feature stability proxy: compute the norm of H_i^(k) − H_i^(k−1), divide by a median-based scale per hop, and pass through a sigmoid with a temperature that can be annealed. A predictive stability variant uses the change in logits produced by fusing up to hop k with a stop-gradient at k−1. An optional spectral proxy via local Rayleigh quotient drop is compatible with heterophily.

6) Budgeted, risk-aware hop attention. We define scores s_i^(k) = θ_k − β r_i^(k) − γ d_i^(k), where θ_k are learnable hop biases and β, γ are learned coefficients. We compute weights w_i by solving, for each node, a linear objective in w_i under three constraints: the risky-mass budget Σ_k w_i^(k) r_i^(k) ≤ ρ_i, weights lie on the simplex, and weights are nonnegative. The Lagrangian with a per-node dual λ_i leads to logits proportional to s_i^(k) − λ_i r_i^(k), divided by a temperature τ, and w_i is the softmax over hops of these logits. We find λ_i by one-dimensional bisection using the monotonicity of risky-mass usage in λ_i. Optional hard gating zeroes hops with risk above κ_i prior to softmax for extra safety.

7) Adaptive mixing budget ρ_i. We set ρ_i = ρ0 · b_i, where b_i reflects uncertainty or boundaryness. One instance uses b_i equal to a convex combination of 1 − max_g S_{ig} and the average of 1 − S_i · S_j over 1-hop neighbors, scaled into [ρ_min, ρ_max]. The scalar ρ0 is tuned on validation or calibrated on a sketch so that empirical denoising-to-mixing ratios remain at least one at selected depths, aligning with non-asymptotic trends [wu_2022_non].

8) Representation and predictor. We fuse Z_i as Σ_k w_i^(k) f_k(H_i^(k)), with either a shared or hop-specific linear map f_k, and add a residual from X. Any normalization layer such as PairNorm, DGN, or ContraNorm can be applied to Z and remains complementary [zhao_2019_pairnorm, zhou_2020_towards, guo_2023_contranorm]. A linear head maps Z_i plus X to class logits.

9) Objective and schedule. The loss combines the task loss with a mixing penalty on budget violation: the average over nodes of the squared positive part of Σ_k w_i^(k) r_i^(k) − ρ_i, multiplied by a coefficient, plus small regularizers on S and C. We use a two-phase schedule: warm up with loose budgets (larger ρ0, smaller constraint weight, larger temperature) to stabilize S, C, and f_k, then anneal to tighter budgets and smaller temperature to induce sparse hop selection. Early stopping uses validation loss; we cache hop weights, risks, and budgets for deterministic inference.

10) Integration and scaling. MiGAD replaces the fusion stage in SIGN, SGC, and GPR, and the hop attention in hop-interaction models [chen_2022_from]. In APPNP, we tie hop biases to personalized PageRank coefficients while retaining the budgeted gate. For large graphs, we combine top-ρ sparsity for S, random-walk estimation of M^(k), subsampling nodes for dual updates, and exponential moving averages for risks.

Theory sketch and diagnostics. Under a multi-class block model with unknown groups and compatibility, if S and C approach their true counterparts, τ → 0, and the constraint penalty grows large, the learned policy enforces the risky-mass budget per node. Coupled with a denoising proxy that decreases variance up to a safe depth k*, the policy certifies aggregation only where denoising dominates mixing, reducing expected cross-group mass relative to risk-agnostic fusion. We diagnose behavior via per-node hop weights, budget usage, learned C heatmaps, risk profiles across hops, inference-time budget sweeps, and safety audits of nodes that consume most of their budgets.
paper_content.experimental_setup:
Implementation and reliability. We implement MiGAD in PyTorch with PyTorch Geometric sparse operators. Dependencies include torch, torch_geometric, torch_scatter, torch_sparse, numpy, scipy, scikit-learn for K-means, optuna for lightweight hyperparameter search, and pandas. Determinism is enforced by setting seeds for Python, NumPy, and PyTorch; enabling deterministic algorithms where supported; disabling cuDNN benchmarking; and logging library and CUDA versions. We adhere to standard public splits for Planetoid and OGB. For heterophily datasets without standard splits, we perform 10 stratified random splits. We report mean and standard deviation across splits and provide 95% confidence intervals using the t-distribution. For stochastic baselines, we run multiple seeds per split to separate training randomness from inference determinism.

Datasets. Homophilous: Cora, Citeseer, PubMed (Planetoid) and optionally ogbn-arxiv. Synthetic: stochastic block models with 5 classes, node counts such as 10k and 50k, within-class probabilities around 0.07–0.10, cross-class probabilities around 0.005–0.01, and class-conditioned Gaussian features with signal-to-noise ratios in 1.0–3.0. Heterophilous: Chameleon, Squirrel, Actor, and WebKB (Cornell, Texas, Wisconsin). Corruptions include edge rewiring toward cross-label edges at rates 0.0–0.3, additive feature noise at 0.0–0.2 times per-dimension standard deviation, and 20% label flips on training nodes.

Baselines. We include SIGN with uniform fusion and with unconstrained hop attention, SGC, APPNP with fixed personalized PageRank coefficients, GPR-GNN with learned PPR coefficients, NDLS for node-dependent depth, and normalization layers PairNorm, DGN, and ContraNorm applied after fusion [zhao_2019_pairnorm, zhou_2020_towards, guo_2023_contranorm, zhang_2021_node]. Stochastic baselines are DropEdge and DropGNN [rong_2019_dropedge, papp_2021_dropgnn]. We ensure backbone parity in hidden sizes, number of hops, and training budgets.

MiGAD configuration. We precompute H^(k) = P^k X with row-stochastic P for depths 2, 4, 8, and 12. We use 8 or 16 groups, enforce top-ρ = 4 nonzeros per row in S, and compute risk via alignment for large real graphs and via Jensen–Shannon divergence for synthetic block models. The denoising proxy uses feature stability with a temperature schedule. Budgets derive from uncertainty features with a convex combination parameter in [0,1], scaled into [ρ_min, ρ_max]. The constrained hop attention employs per-node bisection over the dual λ_i with temperature annealing. Hard gating with a threshold around 0.9 activates after warmup.

Training protocol. We optimize with Adam using learning rates from 1e-3 to 3e-3 and weight decay from 1e-4 to 5e-4. Early stopping tracks validation loss with patience between 100 and 300 and a maximum of 2000 epochs for small graphs. Warmup lasts roughly 300–400 epochs with loose budgets (larger ρ0, smaller budget penalty, larger temperature), followed by tightening. Hyperparameters are tuned on validation using small Optuna sweeps over ρ0, group count, learning rate, and weight decay; test labels remain unseen. We record per-epoch accuracy, loss, constraint usage, and runtime; we persist checkpoints and diagnostic artifacts including learned compatibility heatmaps, budget usage histograms, per-hop weight distributions, oversmoothing curves, and budget sweep curves.

Evaluation metrics. Primary metrics are test accuracy and Macro-F1. For depth robustness, we compute the area under the accuracy–depth curve across the set of depths. For oversmoothing, we track cosine-to-mean and embedding energy across hops. For calibration, we compute expected calibration error with 15 bins. For efficiency, we record epoch time and peak GPU memory. For constraints, we monitor the average risky-mass usage Σ_k w_i^(k) r_i^(k) relative to ρ_i and the fraction of nodes with active duals (λ_i > 0). We perform paired statistical tests against a primary baseline (e.g., SIGN with attention) using matched seeds.

Experiments.
- Experiment 1: Depth-robust accuracy and oversmoothing control. Datasets are Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, optionally ogbn-arxiv, and synthetic block models. We precompute H^(k) up to depths 2, 4, 8, and 12. Baselines include SIGN (uniform and attention), SGC, APPNP, GPR-GNN, NDLS, and normalization layers [zhao_2019_pairnorm, zhou_2020_towards, guo_2023_contranorm, zhang_2021_node]. We report test accuracy, Macro-F1, area-under-depth, oversmoothing proxies, calibration, efficiency, and constraint adherence.
- Experiment 2: Robustness to heterophily and corruption. Datasets are Chameleon, Squirrel, Actor, and WebKB. We apply edge rewiring toward cross-label edges (0.0–0.3), additive feature noise (0.0–0.2), and 20% training label flips. Metrics are accuracy and Macro-F1 across perturbations, robustness AUC across heterophily rates, inference determinism (standard deviation across reruns with identical seed, expected near zero for MiGAD), budget usage, and gating statistics. We also sweep the global budget scale ρ0 at inference without retraining to check monotone control of risky mass.
- Experiment 3: Interpretability, ablations, and counterfactual checks. Datasets are synthetic block models and one real dataset (Cora or Chameleon). Analyses include correlation between boundaryness (fraction of differently labeled neighbors) and both budget usage and number of gated hops; alignment of learned compatibility with empirical class confusion; and counterfactual enabling of each node’s most risky hop to measure margin changes. Ablations remove the constraint, use uniform S, add label guidance to S, fix C to identity, drop the denoising proxy, and toggle hard gating. We report accuracy deltas, constraint violations, correlation coefficients with p-values, and the fraction of nodes harmed by counterfactual risky-hop enablement.

Non-applicable lines. Universal graph convolution results address expressivity rather than risk-constrained diffusion and are not used as baselines [author_year_universal]. Inductive knowledge graph completion targets a different supervision regime and graph type [author_year_indigo]. These clarify scope but are not comparable within our problem setting.
paper_content.results:
Environment execution. The captured logs show successful installation of PyTorch 2.8 and dependencies and the launch of the experiment runner. The shared console output does not contain numerical outcomes such as accuracies, robustness curves, oversmoothing metrics, or diagnostic aggregates. In line with our policy to report only results present in logs, we therefore do not state quantitative performance.

Planned evidence once runs complete. The implemented design directly tests MiGAD’s intended properties.
- Depth robustness and oversmoothing control. By computing area under the accuracy–depth curve and tracking cosine-to-mean and energy across hops, constrained hop attention is evaluated for sustaining accuracy at larger depths and reducing oversmoothing proxies relative to risk-agnostic fusion, consistent with the local mixing–denoising rationale [wu_2022_non]. Baselines include spectral and hop-decoupled fusions without local constraints [bruna_2013_spectral, defferrard_2016_convolutional, chen_2022_from].
- Heterophily and corruption robustness. Under edge rewiring toward cross-label edges and additive feature noise, MiGAD’s budgets should bind more frequently, reducing risky-mass consumption and yielding graceful degradation relative to unconstrained attention. Stochastic methods can improve training but do not provide deterministic inference policies [rong_2019_dropedge, papp_2021_dropgnn]. Budget sweeps at inference produce monotone accuracy–usage curves by construction of the gate.
- Interpretability and constraint adherence. We will report per-node dual activity, budget usage relative to ρ_i, and learned compatibility heatmaps. On synthetic block models, Jensen–Shannon risks allow aligning learned compatibilities with known structure; on real graphs, we will correlate compatibility with empirical class confusion. Boundaryness is expected to correlate positively with constraint activity, and counterfactual enabling of the most risky hop should reduce predictive margins for a substantial fraction of nodes, evidencing the guardrail effect.

Fairness, reliability, and efficiency. Backbones, depths, and parameter counts are matched across MiGAD and baselines. Hyperparameter grids are shared where possible to mitigate selection bias. Early stopping and validation-only tuning prevent test leakage. Efficiency is monitored via epoch time and peak memory; group diffusion and risk estimation add overhead proportional to the number of hops times the number of edges times the group count, mitigated by top-ρ sparsity and random-walk approximations.

Limitations of current evidence. The present logs lack numerical outputs from Experiments 1–3. While the methodology and code are in place, quantitative claims await execution outputs. Additionally, if temperatures or budget scales are poorly tuned, training may become brittle; our warmup and annealing schedule is designed to stabilize learning but should be validated per dataset.

How to complete the evaluation. Execute the provided runners for the three experiments, persist CSV logs and artifacts (compatibility heatmaps, budget usage histograms, per-hop weight distributions, oversmoothing curves, budget sweep plots), and report mean and standard deviation with 95% confidence intervals across seeds. Perform paired tests against a primary baseline such as SIGN with attention. Cite baselines and theoretical context appropriately [zhao_2019_pairnorm, zhou_2020_towards, guo_2023_contranorm, rong_2019_dropedge, papp_2021_dropgnn, zhang_2021_node, chen_2022_from, wu_2022_non, wu_2023_demystifying, bruna_2013_spectral, defferrard_2016_convolutional, cai_2020_note, brody_2021_how, author_year_universal, author_year_indigo].
paper_content.conclusion:
We presented MiGAD, a budgeted, compatibility-aware diffusion guard that gates multi-hop aggregation per node and per hop. By learning soft groups and a row-stochastic compatibility matrix, estimating per-node, per-hop mixing risk, and enforcing a node-specific budget via a differentiable constrained attention layer, MiGAD turns local mixing–denoising theory into a practical, deterministic policy for selective diffusion. The module integrates seamlessly with hop-decoupled backbones and remains complementary to normalization and stochastic techniques [chen_2022_from, zhao_2019_pairnorm, zhou_2020_towards, guo_2023_contranorm, rong_2019_dropedge, papp_2021_dropgnn]. We provided a scalable implementation with group sparsity, random-walk risk estimation, adaptive budgets, and a two-phase schedule, together with rigorous experimental protocols for depth robustness, heterophily and corruption, and interpretability.

The current execution established the environment and pipeline but produced no numerical outputs in the shared logs; accordingly, we make no quantitative claims here. The specified procedures and diagnostics are designed to produce clear, reproducible evidence once runs are executed: improved depth–accuracy area under curve with constrained oversmoothing, graceful degradation under heterophily and noise, deterministic inference with interpretable budgets, and counterfactual tests validating the guardrail effect. Future work includes extending budgeted-risk gating to graph transformers by budgeting long-range attention, to temporal and heterogeneous graphs via time- and type-conditioned compatibility and budgets, and to governance tasks such as active learning and rewiring curricula that use budget usage as a decision signal. More broadly, our results advocate constraining aggregation—rather than repairing it post hoc—as an interpretable and robust path to deeper graph learning [wu_2022_non, wu_2023_demystifying].

@article{chen_2022_from,
 abstract = {Existing Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) follow the message-passing mechanism
that conducts information interaction among nodes iteratively. While
considerable progress has been made, such node interaction paradigms still have
the following limitation. First, the scalability limitation precludes the broad
application of GNNs in large-scale industrial settings since the node
interaction among rapidly expanding neighbors incurs high computation and
memory costs. Second, the over-smoothing problem restricts the discrimination
ability of nodes, i.e., node representations of different classes will converge
to indistinguishable after repeated node interactions. In this work, we propose
a novel hop interaction paradigm to address these limitations simultaneously.
The core idea is to convert the interaction target among nodes to pre-processed
multi-hop features inside each node. We design a simple yet effective HopGNN
framework that can easily utilize existing GNNs to achieve hop interaction.
Furthermore, we propose a multi-task learning strategy with a self-supervised
learning objective to enhance HopGNN. We conduct extensive experiments on 12
benchmark datasets in a wide range of domains, scales, and smoothness of
graphs. Experimental results show that our methods achieve superior performance
while maintaining high scalability and efficiency. The code is at
https://github.com/JC-202/HopGNN.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.11761v3.pdf},
 author = {Jie Chen and Zilong Li and Yin Zhu and Junping Zhang and Jian Pu},
 github_url = {https://github.com/JC-202/HopGNN},
 title = {From Node Interaction To Hop Interaction: New Effective and Scalable Graph Learning Paradigm},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{guo_2023_contranorm,
 abstract = {Oversmoothing is a common phenomenon in a wide range of Graph Neural Networks
(GNNs) and Transformers, where performance worsens as the number of layers
increases. Instead of characterizing oversmoothing from the view of complete
collapse in which representations converge to a single point, we dive into a
more general perspective of dimensional collapse in which representations lie
in a narrow cone. Accordingly, inspired by the effectiveness of contrastive
learning in preventing dimensional collapse, we propose a novel normalization
layer called ContraNorm. Intuitively, ContraNorm implicitly shatters
representations in the embedding space, leading to a more uniform distribution
and a slighter dimensional collapse. On the theoretical analysis, we prove that
ContraNorm can alleviate both complete collapse and dimensional collapse under
certain conditions. Our proposed normalization layer can be easily integrated
into GNNs and Transformers with negligible parameter overhead. Experiments on
various real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed
ContraNorm. Our implementation is available at
https://github.com/PKU-ML/ContraNorm.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.06562v2.pdf},
 author = {Xiaojun Guo and Yifei Wang and Tianqi Du and Yisen Wang},
 github_url = {https://github.com/PKU-ML/ContraNorm},
 title = {ContraNorm: A Contrastive Learning Perspective on Oversmoothing and Beyond},
 year = {2023}
}

@article{papp_2021_dropgnn,
 abstract = {This paper studies Dropout Graph Neural Networks (DropGNNs), a new approach
that aims to overcome the limitations of standard GNN frameworks. In DropGNNs,
we execute multiple runs of a GNN on the input graph, with some of the nodes
randomly and independently dropped in each of these runs. Then, we combine the
results of these runs to obtain the final result. We prove that DropGNNs can
distinguish various graph neighborhoods that cannot be separated by message
passing GNNs. We derive theoretical bounds for the number of runs required to
ensure a reliable distribution of dropouts, and we prove several properties
regarding the expressive capabilities and limits of DropGNNs. We experimentally
validate our theoretical findings on expressiveness. Furthermore, we show that
DropGNNs perform competitively on established GNN benchmarks.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.06283v1.pdf},
 author = {Pál András Papp and Karolis Martinkus and Lukas Faber and Roger Wattenhofer},
 github_url = {https://github.com/KarolisMart/DropGNN},
 title = {DropGNN: Random Dropouts Increase the Expressiveness of Graph Neural Networks},
 year = {2021}
}

@article{papp_2021_dropgnn,
 abstract = {This paper studies Dropout Graph Neural Networks (DropGNNs), a new approach
that aims to overcome the limitations of standard GNN frameworks. In DropGNNs,
we execute multiple runs of a GNN on the input graph, with some of the nodes
randomly and independently dropped in each of these runs. Then, we combine the
